message:

3 issue rows modified (replace-in-place), no additions or removals.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-bvrjc","title":"tmux split command failure leaves pending split future unresolved","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:17:18.852994Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:33:39.856264Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:33:39.855284Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 65094825. Verified via FT_LOCAL_TARGET=/tmp/ft-rv-p8-bvrjc-target ./scripts/cargo-local.sh test -p mux --lib split_command_error_resolves_pending_split_with_error -- --nocapture. Full mux --lib remains red on ft-3ek7f (domain::tests::local_domain_spawn_pane_first_poll_stays_non_blocking).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-bx4le","title":"[bug] SignalDeduplicator silently behaves like capacity=1 when dedup_capacity=0 (connector_inbound_bridge)","description":"# Bug

connector_inbound_bridge.rs:338 SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record:

    if self.entries.len() >= self.capacity {
        self.entries.pop_front();
    }
    self.entries.push_back(...);

With `dedup_capacity = 0`:
  1. Entry 1: len=0 >= 0 true → pop_front (no-op on empty) → push_back → entries=[a]
  2. Entry 2: len=1 >= 0 true → pop_front removes [a] → push_back → entries=[b]
  3. ...each call mutates entries but len stays at 1.

Net: dedup silently operates as if capacity=1 (one-slot cache that
replaces on every call), not as "dedup disabled."

# Semantic gap

The `dedup_capacity: usize` config field advertises no special
value. Operator setting `dedup_capacity = 0` in their ft.toml
reasonably expects EITHER:
  - dedup disabled (every signal treated as new)
  - config rejected at load

Instead: dedup cache degrades to a 1-entry replacer. Every signal
is compared against only the immediately-preceding signal. Duplicate
detection is 99.99% broken, but no warning fires.

# Parallel precedent

1ad5c8cc fixed the EXACT same class in ConnectorOutboundBridge:
  `dispatch_queue_capacity == 0` now fails-fast with a distinct
  telemetry label and blocked-action record.

The inbound dedup analog needs the same treatment — short-circuit
on `capacity == 0` and bypass the dedup check, returning `true`
unconditionally (every signal is "new") so the bridge doesn't
silently corrupt its dedup semantics.

# Fix

    pub fn check_and_record(&mut self, correlation_id: &str, now_ms: u64) -> bool {
        // [ft-*] When capacity=0, dedup is effectively disabled —
        // skip all tracking and treat every signal as new. Without
        // this, the `len >= 0` check below degrades the cache to
        // a 1-slot replacer rather than honoring the "no dedup"
        // intent.
        if self.capacity == 0 {
            return true;
        }
        self.evict_expired(now_ms);
        // ... existing logic ...
    }

One test: `signal_deduplicator_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup` —
push N entries with same correlation_id, assert all return `true`
and `len() == 0`.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:33:16.059276Z","created_by":"pane3-inbound-zero","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:16.478614Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:16.478198Z","close_reason":"Landed in 8fdfd114 (fix: six independent hardening fixes … connector_inbound_bridge zero-capacity dedup bypass). SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record now short-circuits with true when capacity=0, preserving the 'dedup disabled' semantic operators expect. Companion regression test dedup_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup pins 5-call same-id + multi-id paths. Verified: 1 passed / 0 failed / 22001 filtered (cargo test --lib --features asupersync-runtime -- dedup_zero_capacity), EXIT=0. Parallel to 1ad5c8cc outbound zero-capacity fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connector","inbound-bridge","zero-capacity"]}
 {"id":"ft-c17iw","title":"semantic_anomaly::{Semantic,Conformal}AnomalyDetector::observe allows NaN/Inf embeddings to permanently poison centroid","description":"Both observe() methods normalize the input embedding via normalize_simd, which returns the input unchanged when mag_sq is NaN (NaN > 0.0 is false). A single NaN or Inf component in the embedding flows through normalize → dot_product → distance (masked to 0.0 by f32::max) but the raw normalized_emb is then fed into the EWMA centroid update (mul_add). NaN propagates into every centroid component, renormalize preserves the NaN (because normalize_simd also fails closed to passthrough), and every subsequent observe() sees similarity=NaN → distance=0.0 → anomaly detection silently disabled forever. Fix: reject embeddings with any non-finite component at observe() entry (before centroid mutation). Matches the fail-closed pattern of ft-b4l62 (Histogram), ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure), ft-0p5q5 (EValueMonitor::observe).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:54.255558Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:28:39.706659Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:28:39.706383Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-c1lfg","title":"[mcp] wa.mission_state mission_state filter miss still returns a success mission payload","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:3696-3725, wa.mission_state checks params.mission_state and, when the active mission lifecycle state does not match, still returns McpEnvelope::success with the mission identity, lifecycle_state, counters, and assignments: [] rather than signaling a filter miss. The filter therefore behaves like a placeholder empty-result shim instead of a real selection gate.\n\nImpact: callers asking for a specific lifecycle state receive a success payload for a non-matching mission and must reverse-engineer the mismatch from the returned lifecycle_state field. This makes the advertised filter semantically misleading and complicates conformance/golden expectations.\n\nFix direction: either treat mission_state as a real filter that returns an explicit no-match shape/flag, or reject the request with a targeted error when the active mission does not satisfy the requested lifecycle state. Add coverage for the mismatch path.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:33:25.019931Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:33:25.019931Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-c1lfg","title":"[mcp] wa.mission_state mission_state filter miss still returns a success mission payload","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:3696-3725, wa.mission_state checks params.mission_state and, when the active mission lifecycle state does not match, still returns McpEnvelope::success with the mission identity, lifecycle_state, counters, and assignments: [] rather than signaling a filter miss. The filter therefore behaves like a placeholder empty-result shim instead of a real selection gate.\n\nImpact: callers asking for a specific lifecycle state receive a success payload for a non-matching mission and must reverse-engineer the mismatch from the returned lifecycle_state field. This makes the advertised filter semantically misleading and complicates conformance/golden expectations.\n\nFix direction: either treat mission_state as a real filter that returns an explicit no-match shape/flag, or reject the request with a targeted error when the active mission does not satisfy the requested lifecycle state. Add coverage for the mismatch path.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:33:25.019931Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:51:18.381660Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:51:18.380953Z","close_reason":"Fixed in cec75f71: wa.mission_state now rejects lifecycle-state filter misses instead of returning a success payload for the wrong mission; added mission_state mismatch regression in mcp_tools.rs. Verification partial: local cargo lanes hung after compile/warnings.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-cbeyl","title":"[MEDIUM] mcp_manifest_golden db-gated tool whitelist silently drifts when tool renamed or removed","description":"In commit 1bde42b3 (ft-nnfwn: freeze no-db mcp manifest surface),
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` at
crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_manifest_golden.rs:254-316 hard-codes
three whitelists of tools/resources/templates expected to be db-gated:

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [
    \"wa.accounts\",
    \"wa.events\",
    \"wa.reserve\",
    \"wa.release\",
    \"wa.search\",
    \"wa.send\",
    \"wa.workflow_run\",
] {
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

The assertion only checks absence from \`no_db_tools\`. If a tool is
renamed (e.g., \`wa.accounts\` → \`wa.list_accounts\`) or removed entirely,
the whitelist entry silently stays true (\`!no_db_tools.contains(\"wa.accounts\")\`
is trivially true when \`wa.accounts\` no longer exists in either manifest).

The same drift pattern applies to the resource whitelist
(lines ~285, \`wa://accounts\`, \`wa://events\`, \`wa://reservations\`) and
the template whitelist (lines ~295, \`wa://accounts/{service}\` etc.).

Impact: the no-db subset guard gives false confidence. A tool could
accidentally become always-on (or be removed from db-gated registration)
without the test catching it.

Fix: for each whitelist entry, assert \`full_tools.contains(entry)\`
BEFORE checking \`!no_db_tools.contains(entry)\`. That way, if the
whitelisted tool disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test
fails and forces whoever made the change to update the whitelist
deliberately.

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [...] {
    assert!(
        full_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"whitelist drift: {expected_absent_tool} no longer exists in db-backed manifest — update the test\"
    );
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

Severity rationale: medium — test-quality issue that masks regressions
in MCP tool surface categorization. Not a correctness bug in production
code.

Evidence: commit 1bde42b3 diff lines 262-316 of the new
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` test body.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:24:49.960054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915565Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915363Z","close_reason":"Added full_*.contains(X) pre-assert for each whitelisted tool/resource/template before the !no_db.contains(X) check. If a whitelisted identifier disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test now fails instead of passing trivially. Fix landed in 4d52f550. Verified 6/6 tests pass via rch exec.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","review","test-gap","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-cuzeu","title":"backpressure small-capacity queues fail closed to Black via saturating_sub saturation rule","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:39:42.915949Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:08:01.032259Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:08:01.032016Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-d0ez0","title":"[EPIC] End-to-end swarm integration proof: demonstrate full observe-detect-react loop with live agents","description":"# Goal
Prove that FrankenTerm actually delivers on its core value proposition end-to-end: observing live AI agent panes, detecting state transitions, triggering automated workflows, and exposing actionable data through Robot Mode. Today every subsystem has unit tests, but nobody has proven the aggregate works.

# Background
The reality check (2026-04-06) found that individual subsystems are well-implemented:
- Ingest pipeline: real delta extraction with 4KB overlap matching
- Pattern detection: real Aho-Corasick + Bloom + regex
- Storage: real SQLite v23 + FTS5
- Robot Mode: fully implemented
- Policy engine: 40+ subsystems
- Fleet memory: 4-tier backpressure (Normal/Elevated/Critical/Emergency)

But there is NO evidence that someone has run: ft watch -> observed real panes -> detected a real pattern -> triggered a real workflow -> verified via ft robot. The individual subsystems are proven; the integration is not.

# Why This Matters
FrankenTerm promises a swarm-native terminal platform. Without E2E proof, it is a collection of well-tested libraries, not a platform.

# Architectural Intent
1. Start with a minimal proof: 3 panes, synthetic agent output, verify Robot Mode sees detection events.
2. Scale to realistic: 20 panes with mixed agent types, workflow triggers, policy gates, FTS search.
3. Scale to stress: 50+ panes with fleet memory engaged, prove tiered scrollback works under load.
4. Prove tx execution: ft tx run against real panes with policy gates and compensation.
5. For each scale tier, demonstrate the FULL loop: observe -> detect -> react -> audit.

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Minimal E2E (ft-d0ez0.1): ft watch + 3 panes + pattern detection + Robot Mode queries
2. 20-pane integration (ft-d0ez0.2): multi-agent detection, workflow, policy, FTS search
3. 50-pane stress (ft-d0ez0.3): fleet memory pressure transitions (Normal->Elevated->Critical), tiered scrollback eviction
4. Tx E2E (ft-d0ez0.4): ft tx run with real pane operations, compensation, audit trail
5. All tests produce structured JSON artifacts for reproducibility.
6. Tests run in CI or have clear manual reproduction instructions.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:56:35.214581Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:06:45.893656Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:06:45.893210Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","goal-line","integration","proof","swarm"]}
@@ -276,7 +276,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-rfpk6","title":"session_restore + snapshot_engine open_conn helpers omit foreign_keys PRAGMA — cleanup paths leak orphan rows","description":"ft-s4myu fixed StorageHandle::with_config to enable foreign_keys on every connection open. But two OTHER SQLite writers in frankenterm-core have their own open_conn helpers that set journal_mode and busy_timeout but skip foreign_keys:

  - crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:254 — used by delete_session, load_checkpoint_by_id, list_sessions, show_session, session_doctor, and 5+ other production paths.
  - crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:1293 — used by cleanup_sync which does 'DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE checkpoint_at < ?1' and 'DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE id IN (...)'. The mux_pane_state → session_checkpoints FK with ON DELETE CASCADE at schema line 646 is supposed to cascade the child deletion. With FKs OFF, cleanup silently leaves orphan mux_pane_state rows.

Fix: add 'PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON' to both open_conn helpers. Regression test: run snapshot_engine::cleanup against a populated DB, assert no orphaned mux_pane_state rows remain.

Scope: same shape as ft-s4myu (compare session_retention.rs:287 which correctly issues the pragma).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:57:28.078383Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:59:41.453779Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:59:41.453482Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-riz42","title":"Migrate wezterm-client and frankenterm-mux-server-impl from direct smol to asupersync","description":"Both crates have direct smol::Async<T>, smol::channel, smol::future::or, and async_io::IoSafe usage that needs asupersync equivalents. Key challenge: smol::Async<T> wraps blocking FDs with reactor - asupersync uses capability-based IoCap model instead. Needs design work for the I/O wrapping layer. Blocked by: understanding asupersync IoCap API and creating an Async<T>-like adapter.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmCreek","created_at":"2026-04-02T21:02:11.906232Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T03:56:01.015173Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T03:56:01.014992Z","close_reason":"Completed direct Smol removal in wezterm-client and frankenterm-mux-server-impl; remaining debt tracked in ft-xbnl0.2.1 verification finish-line work","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2733,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"jemanuel","text":"TopazHarbor research: asupersync uses IoRegistration (per-type, not generic Async<T>). TcpStream/UnixStream each embed Option<IoRegistration> directly. To build generic Async<T>: wrap T:AsRawFd + IoRegistration + implement AsyncRead/AsyncWrite with rearm pattern. Alternative: add feature-gated conditional compilation (async-smol vs async-asupersync) like SSH crate, keeping smol path until native mux replaces these legacy crates.","created_at":"2026-04-02T21:22:30Z"},{"id":2827,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Code-grounded execution split after validating current sources and Asupersync primitives on 2026-04-03.

What the code says now
- `frankenterm/client` and `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl` are the direct Smol entrypoints.
- Support crates already partially know about native Asupersync:
  - `codec` defaults to `async-asupersync` and only stays on Smol when callers force `async-smol`.
  - `frankenterm-ssh` defaults to `async-asupersync` and already exposes a runtime shim (`runtime::block_on`, channel/io reexports).
  - `frankenterm-uds` and `async_ossl` default to `async-asupersync`, but today they only expose blocking `Read`/`Write` wrappers plus optional `async_io::IoSafe`.
- The hard blockers are therefore not abstract runtime folklore; they are concrete call sites and wrapper gaps:
  1. local Unix / mux-server dispatch path
  2. SSH proxy / pipe path
  3. TLS/OpenSSL path

Important design correction
- The first idea in this bead description about creating an Async<T>-like generic adapter should NOT be the default architecture.
- Asupersync's native pattern is per-type `Option<IoRegistration>` plus `rearm(...)` on `WouldBlock`, as seen in its own `net::unix::UnixStream` and `process` pipe wrappers.
- The finish-line-safe approach is: use concrete per-type native adapters where the project owns the type, and only fall back to a narrow local adapter when there is no better owned boundary.

Recommended implementation slicing
1. Local Unix + mux-server dispatch first.
   files: `frankenterm/uds/src/lib.rs`, `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs`, `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, relevant `Cargo.toml` files.
   plan: add native Asupersync `AsyncRead`/`AsyncWrite` + readiness registration to `frankenterm-uds::UnixStream`; stop forcing `codec`/`uds`/`async_ossl` into Smol mode from client and mux-server manifests; replace `smol::Async` + `smol::channel` + `smol::future::or` in dispatch and the local client path with native Asupersync-shaped loops.

2. SSH proxy / pipe path second.
   files: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, possibly `frankenterm/ssh/*` only if needed.
   plan: remove direct `smol::block_on` in favor of the SSH runtime shim immediately; then decide whether `SshStream` should gain a small local Asupersync pipe-registration adapter or reuse an existing process-pipe pattern from Asupersync. This path is not the same problem as Unix sockets and should not be conflated with them.

3. TLS/OpenSSL path third, coordinated with finish-line networking work.
   files: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, `frankenterm/async_ossl/src/lib.rs`.
   plan: either give `AsyncSslStream` a native Asupersync readiness wrapper using the same `IoRegistration`/`WouldBlock` pattern, or deliberately hand this slice to the broader native-network/TLS lane if the correct answer is to stop using this wrapper entirely. Do not hide TLS behind another executor-neutral shim.

Immediate low-risk cleanup opportunities once the first slice starts
- direct `smol::block_on(sess.exec(...))` in `client.rs` should route through `frankenterm-ssh`'s runtime shim rather than calling Smol directly.
- `smol::Timer::after(...)` in `client.rs` should move to a native timer path (`asupersync::time` or a repo-local native shim) rather than survive as a stray holdout.

This bead should now be read as: concrete per-transport native cutover, not "invent a new generic Async<T> compatibility layer".","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:04:26Z"},{"id":2831,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Progress update 2026-04-04.

Completed in-code cleanup before the larger stream-wrapper migration:
- `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`
  - `client_thread(...)` now uses `promise::spawn::block_on(...)` instead of importing Smol `block_on`.
  - SSH exec call sites now use `wezterm_ssh::runtime::block_on(...)` instead of `smol::block_on(...)`.
  - version-compat timeout now uses `promise::spawn::sleep(...)` instead of `smol::Timer::after(...)`.
- `frankenterm/client/src/domain.rs`
  - title propagation debounce now uses `promise::spawn::sleep(...)` instead of `smol::Timer::after(...)`.

This does NOT close the bead. Remaining direct Smol/async-io debt in `client.rs` is still the main work:
- `smol::channel::{bounded, unbounded, Receiver, Sender}`
- `smol::Async`
- `smol::future::or`
- `async_io::IoSafe` bounds for the Unix/SSH/TLS stream path

Coordination note: MCP Agent Mail health is green, but stateful reads/writes are currently failing with `DATABASE_ERROR` / `no such table: projects`, so file reservation and inbox checks are temporarily unavailable from this session.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:08:29Z"},{"id":2834,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Implemented the first native Asupersync transport slice in frankenterm/uds/src/lib.rs: UnixStream now carries feature-gated async-asupersync AsyncRead/AsyncWrite(+vectored) impls backed by lazy nonblocking conversion plus IoRegistration re-arm, without removing the existing blocking Read/Write surface. Added an async roundtrip regression that specifically exercises an initial WouldBlock before data arrives. Remote cargo check caught a broad test ambiguity from importing AsyncReadExt/AsyncWriteExt at module scope; that is fixed by scoping the imports to the new async test, and a fresh rch check is now in flight. This keeps the local Unix lane moving without inventing a generic Async<T) wrapper and sets up the next step as caller cutover in wezterm-client/local mux dispatch or parallel extension of the same pattern to async_ossl for the TLS lane.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:24:55Z"},{"id":2839,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"jemanuel","text":"2026-04-04 progress update.

Verified remotely via rch:
- `rch exec -- cargo check -p async_ossl --all-targets` passed after fixing the dual-trait vectored write ambiguity in `frankenterm/async_ossl/src/lib.rs`.
- `rch exec -- cargo check -p wezterm-client --all-targets` passed after moving `wezterm-client` Unix/TLS transport ownership onto the native wrappers: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs` now boxes raw `UnixStream` and `AsyncSslStream` directly, and `frankenterm/client/Cargo.toml` now requests `wezterm-uds`/`async_ossl` with `async-asupersync` features instead of `async-io`. The SSH proxy path is now the remaining direct `smol::Async` holdout in `client.rs`.

`frankenterm-mux-server-impl` narrowed to one concrete integration issue: `dispatch.rs` erased `wait_for_readable()` behind a non-Send boxed future, which made `crate::dispatch::process(stream)` fail `spawn_into_main_thread` Send bounds from `src/local.rs`. I tightened the dispatch trait to require a Send boxed readiness future and kicked off a fresh `rch exec -- cargo check -p frankenterm-mux-server-impl --all-targets`; result still pending as of this comment.

Design status: the transport slice is now materially smaller and clearer. Local Unix and TLS are on the owned Asupersync-backed stream types; the next native cut after this rerun is the SSH split-FD stream and then the smol channel/or loops.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:58:56Z"},{"id":2840,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"jemanuel","text":"Follow-up verification: the fresh rerun `rch exec -- cargo check -p frankenterm-mux-server-impl --all-targets` passed after tightening `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs` so `DispatchStream::wait_for_readable()` returns a Send boxed future. That clears the local Unix/TLS transport slice across `async_ossl`, `wezterm-client`, and `frankenterm-mux-server-impl`; the remaining direct Smol surface in this bead is now concentrated in the SSH proxy stream plus the smol channel/or loops.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:59:45Z"},{"id":2923,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"CalmCreek","text":"Taking the next concrete slice of ft-riz42: switching wezterm-client and frankenterm-mux-server-impl onto codec async-asupersync, replacing their remaining smol channel/or coordination loops, and removing the SSH smol::Async<SshStream> wrapper in favor of a native split-FD readiness wrapper. I’m reading the filedescriptor/asupersync APIs now before patching.","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:09:27Z"},{"id":2925,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"CalmCreek","text":"Implemented the remaining direct Smol cut in the current ft-riz42 slice. Changes: frankenterm/client and crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl now request codec with async-asupersync; both crates dropped direct smol deps in favor of async-channel plus asupersync; client.rs replaced the smol future/or and channel coordination loop with futures select, replaced the SSH smol Async wrapper with a native split-FD SshStream that implements asupersync AsyncRead and AsyncWrite via IoRegistration, and swapped the version-timeout or-path to select; dispatch.rs replaced smol channel unbounded plus smol future/or with async_channel plus futures select. Verification: rch exec -- cargo check -p wezterm-client -p frankenterm-mux-server-impl --all-targets passed after the borrow and lifetime fixes; broad rch exec -- cargo check --workspace --all-targets is running now. Workspace cargo fmt --check currently reports unrelated pre-existing diffs outside my edit surface, while rustfmt --check passes on the two edited Rust files.","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:36:17Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-rpez9","title":"[reality-gap] README tx examples target a top-level CLI that the shipped ft binary does not expose","description":"README.md documents top-level `ft tx ...` commands in the command matrix and troubleshooting examples, but the shipped binary at `FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft` rejects `ft tx --help` with `error: unrecognized subcommand 'tx'`.

Observed reality:
- `./FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft --help` has no top-level `tx` command.
- `./FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft robot --help` advertises `tx plan|run|rollback|show` under robot mode.
- `./FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft mission plan --contract mission.json` also fails because the live flag is `--mission-file`, not `--contract`.

Impact: an operator following README examples gets an immediate parse failure from the shipped binary.

Expected fix:
- Update README tx examples to the live robot surface (`ft robot tx ...`).
- Use the live flag spelling (`--contract-file`) where a contract path example is shown.
- If a top-level human tx surface is intended, ship it and re-verify the binary before documenting it.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:34:21.541365Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:50:06.280823Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:50:06.280129Z","close_reason":"AS-VERIFIED, no README edit needed. The bead's two concrete gaps were already closed by commit 3b9838ce (docs(readme): correct tx and config examples, same-day):

(1) bare 'ft tx ...' → 'ft robot tx ...' at README:158 (Quick Example tx run), :161 (tx show), :225 (Safety Guarantees bullet), :1230 (FAQ section). Current grep for bare 'ft tx ' matches zero lines.

(2) '--contract' → '--contract-file' at the matching lines. Current grep for '--contract' (not '--contract-file') matches zero lines. Cross-checked against the clap wiring: RobotTxCommands::Plan/Run at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:3173-3184 bind '#[arg(long)] contract_file', matching the README.

Note on bead context: the bead cited './FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft --help' showing no top-level 'tx' subcommand, but the live source at main.rs:430-443 actually defines a top-level 'Tx { command: TxCommands }' variant — that app bundle was stale. Either way the current README uses 'ft robot tx' consistently, which is also valid. No ambiguity left.

Did not touch README.md; respected GreenWren's exclusive reservation (expires 2026-04-23T20:42Z).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-rpjud","title":"[review][LOW] ft-eze48 InflatedDimension wire_mode capped at MAX_DIMENSION=32, misses u16::MAX memory-pressure case","description":"commit 6567397b added a fuzz WireMode::InflatedDimension variant at fuzz/fuzz_targets/ftvi_index_from_bytes.rs:191-196:

    WireMode::InflatedDimension(seed) => {
        if bytes.len() >= 8 {
            let dimension = u16::from((*seed).max(1)) % ((MAX_DIMENSION as u16) + 1);
            bytes[6..8].copy_from_slice(&dimension.to_le_bytes());
        }
    }

seed is u8. dimension = u16::from(seed.max(1)) % 33 → range [1, 32]. This is EQUAL TO OR LESS THAN the MAX_DIMENSION cap already used for generated records, so 'Inflated' is a misnomer — the written dimension is never actually inflated beyond what records could carry.

The intent of InflatedDimension is to test the parser's defense against a header claiming e.g. dimension=65535 with count=1 record — which would naively cause the parser to allocate 65535 * 2 bytes per record = 128 KB per record, scaling to memory-pressure attacks if count is also large.

Current code can never produce that attack shape because dimension is clamped to 32.

Fix (one-line):

    let dimension = u16::from((*seed).max(1)).saturating_mul(1024).max(MAX_DIMENSION as u16 + 1);

OR simpler: use the full u16 range:

    let dimension = u16::from_le_bytes([seed.wrapping_add(33), 0xFF]);

Either lets the parser's allocation-size handling be exercised against hostile headers. If the parser does allocate naively, libfuzzer with AddressSanitizer or memory ceilings would flag OOM crashes.

Compare to the parallel InflatedCount variant at :182-189, which uses saturating_add(extra+1) and thus CAN inflate count toward u32::MAX. Inflated dimension should follow the same pattern.

Severity: LOW — the raw-bytes Arbitrary lane (Input::Raw at :62-65) can generate arbitrary u16 dimension values directly, so this adversarial shape is reachable without the Structured lane. But the Structured lane is meant to BIAS toward hitting the interesting corruption shapes with valid surrounding headers, and an under-strength InflatedDimension dilutes that bias.

Audit round 14, 2026-04-23.  {"id":"ft-rrrn5","title":"[SEV] CommandBuilder env_map_serde silently collides duplicate keys after to_string_lossy","description":"frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs env_map_serde (added in 803356bf) maps BTreeMap<OsString, EnvEntry> -> JSON via OsString::to_string_lossy for keys. Two distinct OsString keys whose non-UTF-8 bytes differ but collapse to the same String under lossy conversion will (1) produce a JSON map containing duplicate keys during serialize (most JSON parsers keep last, silently dropping one entry), and (2) on deserialize the BTreeMap::insert loop overwrites on collision, again silently dropping entries without returning an error. The adapter offers no detection or rejection of this case — a corrupted env set roundtrips to a smaller env set with no diagnostic. Metamorphic property: |deserialize(serialize(m))| == |m| — currently violated for any m with keys that collide under to_string_lossy. Conformance concern: the commit message asserts 'POSIX env names are ASCII-only' but setenv(3) on Linux accepts non-ASCII bytes (LC_*=ja_JP.UTF-8 etc.), and libc-level execve passes raw bytes. Fix by either (a) erroring on lossy conversion in both directions, or (b) serializing as an array of [key_bytes, value] pairs via serde_bytes / base64 so the roundtrip is lossless. At minimum add a proptest that asserts roundtrip preserves map size when two synthesized OsString keys share a lossy-equivalent representation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:02:30.464855Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:35:11.565957Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:35:11.565787Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 7a452b96: replaced map adapter with sequence-of-pairs adapter so every distinct OsString key survives serde roundtrip losslessly (via OsString's native byte/UTF-16-preserving enum encoding in value position). Added 2 regression proptests: one generates arbitrary byte keys (many non-UTF-8) and asserts all distinct keys roundtrip with paired values intact; the other injects two different invalid-UTF-8 sequences that both lossy-render to U+FFFD and asserts both survive. Test-binary run is blocked by pane6-target lock contention; fix is structurally complete and compiles clean at my previous build pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-xv561","title":"[HIGH] pattern-engine regex construction has no backtrack limit — workspace-local ReDoS via user packs","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #2 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
The pattern engine compiles user-supplied regex patterns via
`fancy_regex::Regex::new(raw)` with NO backtrack limit or match
timeout. An attacker who can drop a YAML/JSON pattern pack into
`~/.config/wa/patterns/` or a repo's `.ft/patterns/` directory can
ship a catastrophic-backtracking regex that hangs `ft watch` the
first time matching pane output triggers the pattern's anchor.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:13
  `use fancy_regex::Regex;`
- patterns.rs:512 (RuleDef::validate_regex)
  `Regex::new(regex).map_err(...)` — no `.backtrack_limit(...)`.
- patterns.rs:780 (build_engine_index)
  `let regex = Regex::new(raw).map_err(...)` — same.
- patterns.rs:920 (load_packs_from_config) + :935
  (load_pack_from_id) — pack files are loaded from
  `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` (default
  `~/.config/wa/patterns/`) AND from `.ft/patterns/` inside the
  resolved workspace root. The latter is an attacker-controlled
  directory when a repo is cloned from an untrusted source.
- mcp.rs:2010 (pattern-engine matching hot path)
  invokes `regex.captures_iter(text)` on every segment for every
  rule whose anchor matched. A single-threaded ReDoS blocks the
  matching worker; fancy_regex's default backtracking engine has
  no internal budget.

## Attack vector
1. attacker commits `.ft/patterns/evil.json` with a rule like
   ```json
   {
     "id": "evil.dos",
     "agent_type": "wezterm",
     "event_type": "noop",
     "anchors": ["a"],
     "regex": "^(a+)+$"
   }
   ```
2. user clones repo and runs `ft watch`.
3. the first pane output containing `"aaa...aaab"` (any near-match
   of the anchor) triggers captures_iter, which enters catastrophic
   backtracking. The pattern worker hangs indefinitely, wedging the
   detection pipeline.

No code execution — DoS only. But `ft watch` is the primary
operator surface; wedging the detection loop silently destroys
the "intelligent detection" claim the README makes.

## Fix direction
Two layers:

1. Compilation-time gate (quick):
   - Replace `Regex::new(raw)` with `fancy_regex::RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(1_000_000).build()`
     at patterns.rs:512 and :780. 1M steps is plenty for any
     legitimate rule and caps worst-case match cost.
   - Propagate the resulting `fancy_regex::Error::RuntimeError` from
     `captures_iter` as a `Detection` of kind `RegexRuntimeError`
     instead of hanging.

2. Match-time budget (robust):
   - Wrap each `regex.captures_iter(text)` call in a time-bounded
     harness (e.g., spawn a short-lived asupersync task with a
     100ms timeout). On timeout, quarantine the offending rule and
     surface a "rule disabled due to catastrophic match cost"
     telemetry event so operators can triage.

## Severity rationale
- HIGH (P1): attacker-controlled repo → DoS on user's machine.
- Not CRITICAL: local exploitation only (repo-clone vector), no
  network reach, no data exfil, no code execution.

## Out of scope for this bead
- RE2/Hyperscan migration (much larger rework).
- Per-rule memory budget (separate concern).

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same audit pass
  already fixed the MCP arbitrary-file-read vector.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #2; recommend shipping the
compilation-time gate within one follow-up session.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:04:22.269417Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976852Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976108Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 070117a0: fix(patterns): cap regex backtracking at 10M steps to close ft-xv561 pattern-engine ReDoS on attacker-controlled pattern packs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","patterns","redos","security"]}

-{"id":"ft-xydxz","title":"[review][LOW] ft-eze48 ftvi fuzz corpus has only one seed (FTVI magic + EOF), sparse coverage for libfuzzer starting points","description":"commit 6567397b landed fuzz/fuzz_targets/ftvi_index_from_bytes.rs with a single corpus seed at fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/short_magic. Hex dump confirms it is 5 bytes: 46 54 56 49 0a (the real magic b'FTVI' followed by 0x0a).

The seed uses the correct magic bytes (verified against crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:10 const MAGIC = b'FTVI'). That is the good part — it seeds libfuzzer at the real format boundary, not random garbage.

Gap: only ONE seed, covering only ONE failure shape (magic-matches-but-truncated-at-version). A properly-seeded fuzz target typically ships 5-10 seeds exercising:
- Empty input (zero bytes)
- Minimal valid FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=0 + count=0 (12 bytes total, no records)
- Single-record FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=1 + count=1 + one (u64 id, u16 f16-value) record
- Magic-only (4 bytes — truncates before version)
- Valid magic + valid version + truncated at dimension (6 bytes)
- Valid header claiming count=1 + zero record bytes (parser must not OOB)
- Valid header + bogus version (e.g., version=255) to exercise version rejection

The Structured/Arbitrary lane at :66-84 generates real FTVI buffers at runtime via write_ftvi_vec, so coverage CAN be reached, but libfuzzer's coverage-guided exploration benefits from diverse starting seeds — especially for the header state machine, where coverage-guided mutation is more productive when seeded at valid-header boundaries.

Fix: add 5-7 curated seed files to fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/ constructed via write_ftvi_vec and dd/printf. One-time scripting cost.

Severity: LOW — harness works as-is; this is a corpus-quality improvement, not a correctness gap. Audit round 14, 2026-04-23.

The seed uses the correct magic bytes (verified against crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:10 const MAGIC = b'FTVI'). That is the good part — it seeds libfuzzer at the real format boundary, not random garbage.

Gap: only ONE seed, covering only ONE failure shape (magic-matches-but-truncated-at-version). A properly-seeded fuzz target typically ships 5-10 seeds exercising:
- Empty input (zero bytes)
- Minimal valid FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=0 + count=0 (12 bytes total, no records)
- Single-record FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=1 + count=1 + one (u64 id, u16 f16-value) record
- Magic-only (4 bytes — truncates before version)
- Valid magic + valid version + truncated at dimension (6 bytes)
- Valid header claiming count=1 + zero record bytes (parser must not OOB)
- Valid header + bogus version (e.g., version=255) to exercise version rejection

The Structured/Arbitrary lane at :66-84 generates real FTVI buffers at runtime via write_ftvi_vec, so coverage CAN be reached, but libfuzzer's coverage-guided exploration benefits from diverse starting seeds — especially for the header state machine, where coverage-guided mutation is more productive when seeded at valid-header boundaries.

Fix: add 5-7 curated seed files to fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/ constructed via write_ftvi_vec and dd/printf. One-time scripting cost.

 {"id":"ft-y87lf","title":"pdu_stream_decode ASan allocation-size-too-big on new fuzz artifact","description":"`cargo +nightly fuzz run pdu_stream_decode --features codec-fuzz-targets -- -max_total_time=120 -detect_leaks=0` found a new crash in `codec::Pdu::stream_decode` under ASan.

Artifact: `fuzz/artifacts/pdu_stream_decode/crash-22974e1f96d936743995342b9a12dc33a8af8f51`
Repro: `cargo fuzz run --features=codec-fuzz-targets pdu_stream_decode fuzz/artifacts/pdu_stream_decode/crash-22974e1f96d936743995342b9a12dc33a8af8f51`

Observed failure: `AddressSanitizer: requested allocation size 0x1fffffd2000000 exceeds maximum supported size` followed by `SIGABRT`. This looks like a new oversized-allocation path despite the existing OOM regression coverage in `frankenterm/codec/tests/regression_oom_crasher.rs`.

The short soak also grew corpus coverage substantially before the abort; no other new artifacts were emitted during the run.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:36.123052Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:44:35.881131Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:44:35.880846Z","close_reason":"Bounded ClusteredLine and codec decode allocations; pinned the 22974e fuzz crasher in regression and corpus","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-y9lnb","title":"[EPIC] Transaction execution data-plane: connect orchestration to real pane operations, policy gates, and agent dispatch","description":"# Goal
Bridge the gap between FrankenTerm's well-engineered tx/mission orchestration framework and actual execution against live panes. Today the framework provides type-safe state machines, idempotency ledgers, observability hooks, and resume semantics — but step execution against panes is synthetic. This epic wires the data plane so that ft tx run actually does things.

# Background (reality check 2026-04-06, updated after three verification passes)
- tx_execution.rs (1,663 lines, 28 tests): StepExecutor trait with 3 methods. SyntheticStepExecutor for testing, PolicyPrepareStepExecutor for real gate evaluation (DONE). TxExecutionEngine<E: StepExecutor> orchestrates full lifecycle but is NOT used by the CLI.
- plan.rs (7,760 lines, 123 tests): Core types solid. Gate evaluation real via TxPreparePolicyAuthorizer/TxPrepareApprovalChecker/TxPrepareTargetLookup traits with StorageBacked impls. StepAction enum (line 529) has SendText, WaitFor, AcquireLock, etc.
- tx_idempotency.rs (2,041 lines, 68 tests): Strong implementation. Integrated in TxExecutionEngine.
- mission_loop.rs (5,291 lines, 116 tests): Real evaluation loop but dispatch is not connected.
- tx_observability.rs (1,287 lines, 20 tests): Event taxonomy complete, integrated in TxExecutionEngine.

# Critical CLI Finding (pass 3)
Both ft tx run AND ft robot tx run import:
  tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all as build_robot_tx_prepare_gate_inputs
This means the CLI BYPASSES all policy gates despite SnowyHorse's PolicyPrepareStepExecutor work. The CLI also doesn't use TxExecutionEngine — it manually calls evaluate_prepare_phase / execute_commit_phase with synthetic inputs. The ft-y9lnb.6 bead addresses this.

# What's Done
- Policy gate wiring (ft-y9lnb.2, closed): prepare-phase traits implemented with StorageBacked adapters.

# What Remains
1. PaneStepExecutor: real execute_steps() and execute_compensations() (ft-y9lnb.1)
2. Mission dispatch: MissionDispatchContract -> WorkflowRunner bridge (ft-y9lnb.3)
3. Timeout/backpressure: per-step/phase/tx timeouts + FleetPressureTier (ft-y9lnb.4)
4. Integration tests: 18 scenarios via TxExecutionEngine<PaneStepExecutor> (ft-y9lnb.5)
5. CLI wiring: ft tx run/rollback use TxExecutionEngine + PaneStepExecutor, add --dry-run (ft-y9lnb.6)

# Acceptance Criteria
1. ft tx run executes real pane operations via PaneStepExecutor, not synthetic stubs.
2. Both human CLI and Robot Mode use real policy gates (no more allow_all import).
3. ft tx rollback sends real compensation commands.
4. ft tx run --dry-run validates permissions without executing (new user safety feature).
5. TxExecutionEngine orchestrates the lifecycle (not manual phase calls in main.rs).
6. Every step produces TxObservabilityEvent audit trail + idempotency ledger entries.
7. 18+ integration tests + 3 E2E scenarios prove the full pipeline.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:53:12.486800Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:58:22.449058Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:58:22.448822Z","close_reason":"All 6 child beads closed: .1 PaneStepExecutor, .2 policy gates, .3 mission dispatch, .4 timeouts/backpressure, .5 integration tests (30 tests), .6 CLI wiring. The tx data-plane is fully wired: ft tx run/rollback uses real PaneStepExecutor with policy gates, timeouts, backpressure, and falls back to SyntheticStepExecutor when backend unavailable.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["data-plane","goal-line","mission","tx"]}
 {"id":"ft-y9lnb.1","title":"Implement PaneStepExecutor: connect tx step execution to Robot Mode send/wait-for primitives","description":"# Goal
Create a PaneStepExecutor that implements the StepExecutor trait by dispatching step operations to actual panes via WeztermInterface.

# Critical Design Constraint: Sync Trait Inside Async Runtime
The StepExecutor trait is SYNC (returns Vec<T>). But the entire CLI runs inside rt.block_on(async { ... }) (main.rs line 16248). WeztermInterface methods are async (return Pin<Box<dyn Future>>). Calling block_on() inside an existing block_on() panics (nested runtime).

## Solution: Approach A (RECOMMENDED) — Make TxExecutionEngine async-capable
Add a parallel async trait:

    pub trait AsyncStepExecutor {
        async fn evaluate_gates(&self, contract: &MissionTxContract, now_ms: i64) -> Vec<TxPrepareGateInput>;
        async fn execute_steps(&self, contract: &MissionTxContract, fail_step: Option<&str>, now_ms: i64) -> Vec<TxCommitStepInput>;
        async fn execute_compensations(&self, commit_report: &TxCommitReport, fail_for_step: Option<&str>, now_ms: i64) -> Vec<TxCompensationStepInput>;
    }

Then either:
- Add TxExecutionEngine::execute_async() that takes AsyncStepExecutor, OR
- Make TxExecutionEngine generic over both sync and async executors via a unified trait with async fn

PaneStepExecutor implements AsyncStepExecutor. It can .await WeztermInterface calls directly since the CLI is already in an async context.

SyntheticStepExecutor and PolicyPrepareStepExecutor keep the sync trait. A blanket impl wraps sync executors into async ones.

## Alternative: Approach B — Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking
Call the sync StepExecutor methods from a blocking thread that CAN create a new runtime:
    let result = tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || {
        let rt = RuntimeBuilder::current_thread().build().unwrap();
        rt.block_on(async { handle.send_text(pane_id, &text).await })
    }).await;

This works but adds thread-hop overhead per step. Acceptable for the MVP.

## RECOMMENDATION
Start with Approach B (spawn_blocking) for the MVP — it's simpler and doesn't require changing TxExecutionEngine. Then migrate to Approach A in a follow-up bead if performance matters.

# Verified Types (plan.rs)
- StepAction enum (line 529): SendText { pane_id, text, paste_mode }, WaitFor { pane_id, condition, timeout_ms }, AcquireLock, ReleaseLock, StoreData, RunWorkflow, MarkEventHandled, ValidateApproval, NestedPlan, Custom
- TxStep { step_id: TxStepId, ordinal: usize, action: StepAction, description: String }
- TxCommitStepInput { step_id, success: bool, reason_code, error_code: Option, completed_at_ms }
- TxCompensation { for_step_id, action: StepAction }
- TxCompensationStepInput { for_step_id, success, reason_code, error_code: Option, completed_at_ms }

# WeztermInterface methods (verified)
- send_text(pane_id: u64, text: &str) -> WeztermFuture<()>
- send_text_with_options(pane_id, text, no_paste, no_newline) -> WeztermFuture<()>
- get_text(pane_id: u64, escapes: bool) -> WeztermFuture<String>
- list_panes() -> WeztermFuture<Vec<PaneInfo>>

# Design: PaneStepExecutor
## evaluate_gates(): delegate to PolicyPrepareStepExecutor (sync, no I/O, no nesting issue)

## execute_steps(): iterate contract.plan.steps, for each TxStep match on action:
- SendText { pane_id, text, paste_mode } -> spawn_blocking + block_on(handle.send_text(...))
- WaitFor { pane_id, condition, timeout_ms } -> spawn_blocking + block_on(poll get_text until match or timeout)
- StoreData { key, value } -> StorageHandle (sync, no nesting issue)
- AcquireLock/ReleaseLock -> PaneWorkflowLockManager if available
- Unsupported (RunWorkflow, NestedPlan, Custom, etc.) -> success: false, reason_code: "unsupported_action"
- On fail_step match: inject deterministic failure

## execute_compensations(): same dispatch but iterating contract.plan.compensations

# Unit Tests (12 minimum)
1. execute_steps SendText happy path: 3 steps, MockWezterm send_text succeed -> 3 success inputs
2. execute_steps SendText pane not found: send_text error -> failed input
3. execute_steps WaitFor match: get_text returns matching text -> success
4. execute_steps WaitFor timeout: pattern not found in time -> failed input
5. execute_steps StoreData: key/value stored -> success
6. execute_steps unsupported action: RunWorkflow -> failed with unsupported_action
7. execute_steps fail_step injection: forced failure at named step
8. execute_steps mixed: 3 steps, step 2 fails -> step 3 not executed
9. execute_compensations happy: compensation text sent -> success
10. execute_compensations failure: compensation send fails -> failed
11. execute_compensations empty: no compensations -> empty vec
12. evaluate_gates delegates: same results as PolicyPrepareStepExecutor

NOTE ON TESTS: Unit tests use MockWezterm which is NOT async — mock_wezterm_handle() returns synchronous mocks. Tests can use the simpler approach. The spawn_blocking path is only needed in the production code path where real WeztermInterface is genuinely async.

# Structured Logging
- tracing::info! per step: step_id, action variant, pane_id, duration_ms
- tracing::warn! on failure with error context
- tracing::instrument on execute_steps and execute_compensations

# Acceptance Criteria
1. PaneStepExecutor implements all 3 StepExecutor trait methods.
2. evaluate_gates delegates to PolicyPrepareStepExecutor.
3. execute_steps dispatches real WeztermInterface calls for SendText, WaitFor, StoreData.
4. Works when called from inside rt.block_on(async { ... }) (the CLI context).
5. All 12+ unit tests pass.
6. No unsafe code.
7.
